title: Toxic Relationships
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Toxic Relationships
summary: Scripture calls believers to love broadly but also provides wisdom for recognizing harmful people and the courage to establish distance when a relationship becomes destructive.
body:
The Bible commands love but never commands naivety. Proverbs is blunt about the company we keep: walk with the wise and become wise, but a companion of fools suffers harm [verse:prov.13.20]. Paul echoes this: bad company corrupts good character [verse:1cor.15.33].

Jesus loved everyone but did not entrust himself to everyone. John records that Jesus knew what was in human hearts and therefore did not commit himself to those whose motives were unreliable [verse:john.2.24-25]. Love and trust are not the same thing. You can love a person without granting them access to your inner life.

Paul provides a practical catalogue of people to distance from: those who are lovers of self, brutal, treacherous, and lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God — and he instructs Timothy to avoid such people [verse:2tim.3.2-5]. The instruction is direct: have nothing to do with them.

Proverbs warns about specific toxic patterns. The person who flatters and the one who rages both require caution [verse:prov.29.5]. An angry person stirs up conflict, and association with them teaches their ways [verse:prov.22.24-25].

Scripture does not ask believers to absorb abuse as a form of virtue. Even Jesus walked away from those who sought to harm him before his appointed time [verse:john.10.39]. Sometimes the most loving thing you can do — for yourself and for the other person — is to create distance.

verses.annotation: The company you keep shapes you — foolish companions lead to harm.
Paul's warning: bad company corrupts good character.
Jesus loved broadly but did not entrust himself to everyone — love and trust are distinct.
Paul's list of harmful character traits with the direct instruction: avoid such people.
Do not associate with the easily angered — you will learn their ways.
Flattery is a trap — not all kindness is genuine.
Jesus physically withdrew from those seeking to harm him — distance as wisdom.

scholars.quote: Longman notes that Proverbs repeatedly distinguishes between the wise and the fool not by intelligence but by teachability — the defining trait of a toxic person in wisdom literature is the refusal to receive correction.

Keener observes that Jesus' selective self-disclosure in John 2:24-25 was a deliberate practice — he engaged openly with crowds but reserved intimacy for those whose hearts he could trust, modeling wise relational boundaries.

Schreiner reads Paul's 'avoid such people' in 2 Timothy 3:5 as pastoral realism — maintaining a loving posture does not require maintaining proximity to people whose patterns are consistently destructive.
